speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] on-site interview, Birkenau, Auschwitz. This is January 26. Hold on. [INAUDIBLE] is on, [INAUDIBLE]. This is on site interview with Renee Firestone in Birkenau, Auschwitz, January 26, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Renee Firestone. When I was brought here 50 years ago, my name was Renee Weinfeld. I was brought on these tracks, on a train full of cattle cars, with my parents and with my 14-year-old sister. I am listening to the wind blowing here, and the quiet terrain, and I can still hear the sounds of the masses of people that were pouring out of the cattle cars. I can still hear the cries of children looking for their parents and for parents looking for their children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it is still in my ears. I hear-- I hear this mother yelling, [? Hannale, ?] [? Hannale, ?] where are you? And then I turn back to the cattle car, and I called for my little sister. And I took her off the cattle car. And in seconds, I looked around and my parents were gone. And there we were, the two of us, separated. Our world collapsed. Our life has changed forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Mengele stood in the middle of the road here and pointed people to go to the right and people to the left. I remember my sister and I were going to the right, and as we were coming into the camp through this gate, at one point my sister noticed that my mother is on the other side. And she kept yelling, Mom, Mom! And she wanted to run to her, and I wouldn't let her. And then she just disappeared, and we were marching into [INAUDIBLE] the camp. Maybe you want to follow me in now. It is hard to comprehend the thousands of people that were pouring out of these cattle cars. I can't even describe the scene. It is impossible to describe the scene. On the loud speakers, the Nazis were telling us to leave our belongings behind. They will deliver it into the camp. Of course, we had no idea what the camp meant. We didn't know who Mengele was, who was pointing us one direction and the other. And we left-- the little belongings that we still kept from home, we left it on the railroad station right here. And then my sister and I was walking through this gate. We had no inkling what is awaiting us. We had no idea what this place was, where this place was. We didn't know, even, what country we are in. We went all the way to the end. We walked through all the way to  the end. There is a bath house there. That's where we got undressed. They told us to leave all our belongings, again, in the dressing room. So, then they took the last piece of clothing off my-- our bodies, and we walked into a room, which was the shower room. We never came back into the dressing room. Naked-- in wind-- in windy, cold weather like this, we walked out naked on the other side of the bath house, and we stood there from about mid afternoon till-- till approximately midnight. It is indescribable, the pain, the fear, the hunger. And you know, today I'm here exactly 50 years-- we're going to commemorate, today, the-- the liberation of this camp. I just-- I just can't believe-- I can not believe that I'm still alive, that I lived it through, that it was possible to live something so diabolical through. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I hope that the world will see what is going-- what was going on here, and will comprehend what it really means to humanity, to remember what is going on-- what was going on here. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My mother-- by the time my sister and I came out of the shower, out of the bath house, and went into another barrack where they shaved our heads and-- and gave us a piece of rag to wear-- by the time we went back into the camp, my mother was reduced to ashes. My father disappeared. My whole world, my whole life was ending. From the bath house they brought us back to this camp, here. This was C Lager. This was the Hungarian women's Lager. If you want to follow me, I'll take you to the barracks where I was in. This is the Lager B to C. This was the Hungarian women's camp. I stayed in this barrack. It was barrack 28. You must understand that all these wires were charged with high voltage electricity. That if you just went near these wires, you were killed. Death was around every corner. No matter how you tried to save yourself, it was impossible. Only God could save you here. I remember when I was in this camp I was still with my little sister. We were somewhere in the middle of the block, in a bunk where there were 12 of us squeezed in. We could hardly fit in. With-- in our sleeps, we always had nightmares, but when we woke up, the nightmare-- the reality was worse than the nightmare. Now that I look around and I see how huge this camp is, I-- I just can't comprehend it because when I was here, millions of people were here. This wasn't a vast, empty space like this. There was no grass, mud, or snow, or-- or dry ground, dry frozen ground. We were always sitting in the mud. This wind that you feel here, well, to save ourselves from this wind, wearing this one piece of clothing that we had on, we had to sit around the edges of the barracks. You know, sitting on the edge of the barracks protecting ourselves with the-- with the walls of the barracks, because we were always outdoors. We-- we could come into the barracks only to sleep. There-- there was a small cubicle like this. Probably this was it, which was the kapo's bed-- kapo's room. She slept here. She had a-- she had a cot in it. And in the middle here, where the chimney is, there was a-- there was a brick chimney like [? working ?] through the whole barrack, which, of course, never had any heat in it. Then, six months later, I was separated from my sister, and I was taken into the next camp, which used to be the Czech Lager, which was murdered overnight, the whole Lager. There were parents with children living there, and the whole camp was eliminated one day. I see these pigeons here. Well, let me tell you, there were no pigeons here. Nothing lived here. There were-- there were no birds, not even a rat could survive here. This is a place of-- [CRYING] This was Lucifer's inferno. [CRYING] The chimneys of the-- of the crematoria were visible from here on all sides. They were bellowing fire and soot 24 hours a day, and we knew that it was our brothers and sisters that were being burned.

speaker: interviewee
text: The trains were coming in 24 hours a day, three, four trains a day, and the prisoners were walking through this gate to go to the bath house. And as they were passing by, we used to beg them, throw your bread. You won't have to-- you won't be able to keep it anyway. Just, why don't you throw it to us? We are hungry. And they didn't know what we were talking about. They thought we were crazy. Why should they throw out their food away, to-- for us or to us? You know, tomorrow-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: We will commemorate the day when this ceased to be the-- the-- the place of suffering, and murder, and-- and unspeakable horror to thousands and thousands of my fellow Jews. I can't believe it. I just can't believe it. If these wires, if these bricks could talk, the stories they could tell, the suffering, and the starvation, and the beatings, and the punishments. [CRYING] If there is such a thing as a soul, I hope that my mother and my sister can hear me now. It is their martyrdom that helped me till now to try to help the world, to try to tell the world what happened here. And I want them to hear me say that I will never forget them, and probably soon we will be together again. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't remember, did I tell you that these wires were charged with high voltage electricity? You couldn't come this close to it because it just-- immediately, you were just drawn to the electricity. You were finished. People came to these wires when they could not bear anymore the suffering, and the hunger, and the starvation. Every morning you could see people hanging from these wires, committing suicide, ending-- ending their lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm looking at these today, and-- and it's hard to believe. It's hard to believe that these poles could tell the stories, the things that happened here. It couldn't be true. We keep saying to ourselves, it couldn't be true. Yet, we all are marked. We all have the numbers on our arms, which tells us that, yes, yes, we were here. We were here. And now I can touch these wires and nothing happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I learned something here, but I wonder whether the world learned anything. The way you look around the world today, you wonder, what did we learn from the Holocaust? What did we learn from this place? Why don't people learn to live in peace with each other? Why don't they learn to respect one another? It's unbelievable, unbelievable, that we still can't get along. We still can't live with each other. Look at this place. Look-- look at the size of it. Can you imagine? Can you imagine the millions that perished here? It's hard for me to remember. It's hard for me to comprehend what I experienced here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only hope that will never happened to any people, anywhere in the world. That's all I hope for, and that's all I pray for. Before we-- before the train arrived here, a little further back the train stopped, and everybody was curious where we are. Nobody knew what country, what town. So, one of the boys picked me up on his shoulders and asked me to look out and tell them if I can see anything. And I remember, I saw that we are on a railroad station. There was a-- a train standing there, another train full of people like us. And then, all of a sudden, I saw the sign, and the sign said, Krakow. And without me even thinking, before it even hit my brain what that meant, I yelled back to the cattle car, we are in Krakow. And I remember that out of that silence, all of a sudden like a beehive, everybody started to talk. Everybody panicked. We all of a sudden realized that we are somewhere in Poland, and we knew that Poland is the bad place to be in, because we already heard rumors that in Poland the Nazis were killing the Jews. I remember the train starting to move again, and it-- and the silence returned. And then the silence was, again, broken with the screeching whistle of the train. And from Krakow all the way here the-- the whistle never stopped. Of course, later we found out that that was the signal for the Nazis to prepare the-- the selection place because a-- a-- a transport is coming. These transports were coming in all the time, and-- and that whistle of the train is still in my ears. That's why, when I look around again, and I-- and I see how quiet this place is and how serene it looks, it certainly didn't look like this then. I-- I think this is crematoria number five. I think that my mother was killed here, and most probably my little sister, also. It is pretty hard to visualize what this place looked like before. Somewhere under here there was a gas chamber. And maybe it's here, where the elevator brought up the bodies, and then they were disposed of in the ovens. I don't know what I can say about it. I-- I don't know how I can even speak about it. It is so frightening. It's just something beyond the human understanding. People come here, I see, and they light candles. I've seen a few candles around here, and somebody left a wreath with Jewish color ribbons. Probably someone whose parents also perished here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can anybody believe this, that this happened? But, we must show it, and we must remember it, and we  must show it to the world. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to come back here, also, and I'm gonna light the candle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, I can't even-- I can't even imagine what this place was like. I recently read Dr. [? Neasley's ?] book, and he writes about this place, and-- and it's just unbelievable how many people were murdered here. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at this. Probably here, probably here it was. Of course, the Nazis blew it up before they left, because they didn't want the world to know what this place was all about. Some of this stuff, can you imagine if this-- if this could speak? If this could tell the stories? If the souls could speak here? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I can't-- I can't speak. I can't-- I can't describe it. I can't describe what I feel, either. I-- I don't know whether I don't feel fear more than anything else. Fear for future generations. I pray to God that nothing like this will ever be built again. Look at the size of this. Unspeakable. Here I can do only one thing, pray for my mother's soul, for my sister's soul, and hope that they are resting in peace, because I am not. [CRYING] If you follow me, we're going to go to the pits now. I will show you something else that you won't believe. [CRYING] This is the bath house. This is the bath house. In these big ovens, they were disinfecting the clothes. Not our clothes, because we never changed clothes here. But when they killed the people, then they stripped them and, of course, they disinfected their clothes. Or, if prisoners from the camp were killed, or-- or had to go away, this is where they brought to disinfect the clothes. By the way, I want you to know that this place was filled with lice. Our clothes was always filled with lice, and we had no way of cleaning it. So, you can imagine that-- you can imagine that the-- the Nazis were terribly scared of typhus, which was rampant here. And that's why this is where they brought all our belongings once we were gone. And this also is a place of horror. On the two sides there are two shower rooms where the prisoners were brought and took a shower, and then, we-- naked, we were disposed of on-- on the side of the bath house. There is a space-- when we go out, I will show you-- which was a barrack at one time. And then we went in there, and they shaved our heads, and they sprayed us with DDT, and then finally, they gave us a piece of rag, which was disinfected here. And that's how we entered the camp itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: We no longer looked like human beings. We no longer felt like human beings. It's-- how can I describe that? How can I show you what it looked like? Thousands of women with shaved heads. We looked like comic strips. I remember, I couldn't find my sister. Once we got out of here, I couldn't find my sister, couldn't recognize her. Took me a few hours before I recognized her. I wonder, what are these scratches on the wall? Who knows? Who knows what's here? I-- I remember that we-- we went into it underground dressing room from the outside. And somehow, I don't remember where, we were coming up to the shower room. I see something here that goes deep, but that's just an oven. Let's see what's happening here. Yeah, it's hard for me to recreate, you know, in my mind, how it exactly worked. But somewhere outside there should be an underground passage into a dressing room. And from that dressing room, once we left all our belongings, they told us to be very neat, and to fold our belongings very neatly, and leave it in a pile so that when we come back we can remember exactly where our clothing is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Needless to say, we walked into the shower room and everything was taken away. And we walked out totally stark naked. And it's-- it's just hard to remember. It's hard to believe that it happened. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Also, somewhere here-- I don't know, maybe it was in this corridor, I don't remember-- Dr. Mengele walked in and took me out of the line. And he was with another man, another doctor, and they took me under a light somewhere in one of those rooms-- I don't remember which one-- and questioned me, and asked me why I'm here. Well, first of all, I didn't know who Mengele was. Second of all, I had no idea where I am and why I'm here. So I said, I'm a Jew, that's why I'm here.
